author: @Reman1975
text:
I used to live in a thermal battery.

It was one of a beautiful old line of miners terrace houses that I was renting in Bristol. The single skin stone walls were getting on for 30" thick, and would soak up heat all summer long, then release it as the temperature started dropping. Even during snowy winter periods, as long as you got a blazing fire going for a couple of hours each evening it would top up the heat stored in the internal walls, and stop it feeling cold till mid way through the following evening.

Loved that house, but it was in a REALLY crappy part of the city. I chose to hand back the keys the SECOND time my car got stolen from outside in 6 months. :(

author: @dennisolsson3119
text:
I love the idea of flow batteries. Gravity batteries not as much.

A whole house (10*10*10 meters, to be optimistic) filed with water would store 2.7kWh for each meter it was lifted, so for home storage it is not as feasible. If you have the land on the other hand, it is probably super cheap in the end.

author: @nevertolatetoprepare2802
text:
You mentioned energy storage. In 1976 or 1977, can't remember now, we in the Plasma and Laser Lab at the University of Florida built a self balancing flywheel, ~2 meters in diameter made out of a 550 lb parachute cord with a braided sheath of the same cord.  We got it up to 30,000 rpm in atmosphere.  The unit massed ~50 kilograms.

Lots of energy stored here.

author: @nlabanok
text: As near as I can tell, jellyrolling a battery or supercap is good for minimizing x, y, AND z dimensions... it's therefore good for minimizing retail shelf space and fitting into limited spaces such as EV chassis....and it's good for reducing evaporative area / conserving electrolyte....but for home storage, the first two of those 3 are N/A and if tightly stacked, the 3rd is N/A as well to keep it simple by just building out planar form factor cells, keeping the cell voltage under the electrolysis threshold for the selected electrolyte, & sealing each cell. It is still painstaking to build enough cells to truly support a building's energy needs but jellyrolling is an unnecessary complication. There is also an argument I suppose that jellyrolling reduces the cell's I/O resistance but even that has a solution in the planar format by tailoring the footprint and thickness of the electrode to the cell output power. But yeah, agree completely that breaking through conventional boundary conditions is a necessary part of process to create practical and useful solutions.

author: @inview1
text:
Well, thought I would check up on you Rob, so I asked ChatGPT AI what he thought of your talk:       

QUESTION: 
I heard this old codger give this talk on battery technology. Is he right or has he lost his marbles, and should he be 'put out to pasture'?:   

Batteries, batteries and supercapacitors. Now I've worked in this industry developing batteries and supercapacitors for, I don't know 30 years or something like that. So I get asked an awful lot about how to make a battery super capacitor. Now in their essence, they're actually very simple. It's little more than sticking a copper nail on a zinc nail and a lemon and you'll have enough energy to run a clock. The basic battery is stunningly simple with only five components or a piece of cake to put together. That's never the issue. The issue is if you do that, you won't get a lot of power. You need a lot of them. And I don't mean a lot in terms of a couple of 100 I mean a lot in terms of 1000s of them. Now you have to make that that's challenging because although people are awesome, lots and lots of things. One thing they're not awesome at is doing the same job time and time again. So take this it's a 500 Farad super capacitor, which is not a lot of energy. In there is about four and a half meters of aluminium foil, coated both sides with a bit of carbon and rolled up tank. If you think four and a half meters isn't that much gunk in a strip of Bekoff file from your kitchen cupboards on row four and a half meters. We're trying to roll it up again. Five will get you 10. You either go offline or you make it wrinkly and you just don't roll it. rolling it back up is challenging when it's four and a half meters. Because you could get a machine to do it constantly. But a battery rolling machine will begin at something like 20 to 25,000 pounds to buy one. That's to make one of these because there's an energy store you're going to need a couple of 1000 of these. So you tried doing that time and time again, that it's going to be for a human being physically impossible to do that repetitious tax, you're going to need to buy some machinery or create some machinery in order to do it. So in the world of the batteries, you are the astronaut founder and you have that kind of dedication, then yeah, you're gonna be able to make your own batteries for home storage, expect to spend five to 10 years and somewhere in the region of 100 to 200,000 pounds, and you'll be able to create a battery bank for your house. No worries at all perfectly doable. Anybody who has that qualities is going to be able to do it and you're going to be able to invent new batteries but most people and I hear most people I'm talking about 95 to 99% of people are not going to do that they're going to set off quite happily thinking they can do it. Give it a go make a mess and just go down to the store and buy a battery because the terminal and battery safety pins per unit and then an 18 650 is four pounds per unit essentially expensive per unit. But once you've struggled with this not very much now if you don't believe any of what I've said, this is not an issue for me knock yourself out and go and try it come back in five years time and we'll have a cup of tea and not give you a biscuit commiseration and you're going to have an awful job doing that. Now that seems like a death knell for home storage, but it isn't. One is I'm calling your attention to the fact that when you think of a battery, do not rush to the default condition of this being a battery or batteries and energy store of which it is is one example. It's one way of storing energy. But there are just so many ways of storing energy. The first one that comes to mind is the finished standard battery was in the news recently. It's basically just a big old pile of sand and a thermos. They're heated up when they've got the energy generation and then they'll take the heat out later and that's brilliant when you think that something like 60% of our energy requirement is in space and water heating, the amount we're used to things like lights and electronics is piffling in comparison. There's also gravity batteries and gravity batteries are a huge area that include pumped hydro, the same stuff that we did with the sand wheel and even just dropping rocks down the shaft. There's a building in India where the entire building is powered overnight by for lift shafts which weights in them. Then of course, we've got flywheels which has been actively researched and compressed air or compressed gas whether it's compressed by a compressor or by the pressure of seawater. Then there is a really good alternative electrochemical battery that's very rarely explored in that the low battery all of these work really well in home storage because space is until limitation because in cars space is a limitation to pretty much no good for them. But at their home storage when you don't have that same limitation. There's just a huge range of alternatives for you to explore. 


ANSWER:
Based on the information provided, the old codger seems to have a good understanding of battery technology and the challenges involved in developing and producing batteries and supercapacitors. He explains that while the basic principle of a battery is simple, creating a powerful and efficient battery requires thousands of components that must be manufactured consistently, which is challenging. He suggests that creating batteries for home storage is possible but would require a significant investment of time and money, and that there are many alternative methods of storing energy that should be explored as well. Overall, the old codger's talk seems informative and reasonable.


QUESTION:     
So you are saying that he has not lost his marbles and he should not be put out to pasture yet?


ANSWER:
Based on the information presented in the text, it seems like the old codger in question has a wealth of experience in the battery industry and is providing valuable insights into the challenges of creating batteries and supercapacitors. While his perspective may be daunting for those looking to create their own batteries at home, he does suggest that there are many alternative energy storage options available. Therefore, it would not be fair to say that he has lost his marbles or should be put out to pasture.



QUESTION:
Oh ok. Well I shall tell him what you said. He will be pleased to know.


ANSWER:
I'm glad I could be of help. If you have any more questions or concerns, feel free to ask!

😜😜


Great content Rob, as always.
Thank you 😃

author: @stevensimmonds4683
text: I like the idea of home energy storage say for small home generation. But havent found the cost effective solution yet. For my own pv  project a simple grid tie inverter (£100 600w) to offset home baseline electric consumption seems most cost effective with additional solenoid switch in with a current switch on mains to ensure not exporting back to grid. (Cheap Limiter function.) May need batteries for smooth buffer for generators with non smooth voltage peaks (wind turbine etc) then use 12v battery with inverter.

author: @idoitforyou6473
text:
great vid i think from all the looking and number crunching (not necessarily building) i have done over the years for home generation and storage using cost ,ease ,safety, space, flexibility as factors the sand battery is the most effective solution especially if every property dedicated a couple square meters to it. They can be both slow and fast charged as well as discharged using relatively basic principles 
In times of emergencies (floods, earthquakes, storms, fires etc) they would be more resilient and useful forms of storage i would imagine of course for such an idea to ever become a reality commerce must be put in its place , if only society could develop a non commercial NEED based structure and leave WANTS for the commercial arm of society

I still like the gravity battery best. Here's why.

The "charge " can be held forever.
The discharge curve is flat.

author: @samhorowitz7593
text: Its sooo important that we as a group come up with "diy" power storage, and you have totally reinforced the importance of not jumping to a battery for power storage! I plan to make a work bench for my garage/workshop that is essentially a finished flat covered surface over the top of my stored energy. Like a large container of hot sand inside the workshop for space heating / energy conversion. The big part is the ideas behind the diy methods. Because these will inherently be designed for everyone to replicate at home, therefore defeating the large corporations that want us to buy heat and energy. Even having to buy acids can be difficult and expensive. Having more methods of seebeck and peltier style modules should take over our home climate control needs. I wish we could all hang out and brainstorm together!

author: @aaronsj80
text: I had an idea of combining a gravity battery and a spring battery where you have a concrete weight that you lift to store energy, but additionally, you have a spring pushing down on that concrete weight so that lifting the concrete weight takes much more energy. I'm not quite sure what the lifting mechanism or energy recovery mechanism would look like though.

replies.author: @mark.guitar
replies.text: Simpler to use a bigger, heavier, weight (Tungsten is pricey but...), or just use a really, really big spring! Adding a spring to a weight doubles the complexity and so reduces the efficiency and reliability. Good old KISS!

author: @mrpants8976
text: I'm very in the favor for the diversity in home storage, but I don't like the comment that super capacitors have limited life cycles, yes, if treated incorrectly they do not last long, but so is the same with all other types of storage, if used correctly super capacitors can last over a million cycles.

replies.author: @readypetequalmers7360
replies.text: I've been using lifepo for battery backups and UPS systems for computer and internet systems. They are great, but I have found they don't save money even with dynamic pricing... by shifting power from $0.21/kwh to $0.12/kwh one can't really save enough to overcome the losses... might be able to make $16/year back. With just 1-2kw of solar one could easily use that power without having a battery and save way more $$. Not against batteries since they are good when they are needed... they just aren't cheap.

Ever make "Crystal Cell", frpm Epson Salt, + salt substitute Copper and Magnesium, low amp voltage but sealed and in parallel they can Slow feed "Lasersaber" channel where I saw this, my DIY, stll 1.4V .20Amp 6 years on shelf. Never built a Cluster, but intend to, hook it to capacitor (Smooth current)to charge controller side by side with PV . Trickle through the nighttime, carry through cloudy days.  Just a thought,  Cheer's to Getting off Grid. PV, Wind, Rain(YT Vid, RainGutter water 3-Dprinted, Pelton turbine) and river Hydro.
Electricity from Air videos on YT, also worthwhile low current built that lasts for Decade's, 4 Capacitor 4 Diode, Antenna and Earth ground, 1.5Vper circuit, same old, Series=Volt, parallel =Amp, $2.50 per unit, dozen YT vid's. I built few, they work. Bendini SSG, also DIY circuit for you to consider..See "myth busters lie" vid YT. Good luck on the QUEST FOR 0-Point.

replies.author: @j.christie2594
replies.text:
Never Store Hydrogen Gas. Store Water and Crack it/separate it, on Demand. My first DIY Hydrogen cell,  I had excess gas that ignited, only a Pint volume small PSI, Shook the Building injured my hand and Ear's.
The Shock wave from Hydrogen ignition is severe danger. Just speaking from Experience, on demand Gas, from Stored Electricity.
Sound can crack water, Piezoelectric speaker, done in Japan., works more efficient (20%) than Standard Electrolysis . Just a thought.
